ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code refers to the plant substance broadly; no dedicated dried-leaf form code identified. 'closest_parent' is used as it represents the botanical origin.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of dried coriander leaf is approximately 1–2 grams (about 1 teaspoon), used as a garnish or flavouring in soups, curries, and rice dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Coriander (cilantro) leaves, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 02013)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried coriander (cilantro) leaves used as primary nutrient reference. Input HPB values are consistent with USDA data and are retained where concordant. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a dedicated dried coriander leaf entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source for this raw dried herb.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Herbs and Spices — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate herb category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dried coriander leaf in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous category of dried herbs with high fibre and low available carbohydrate content. At a 2g culinary serving, the glycemic load is negligible (GL ≈ 0.4). Estimation basis: Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables, low-GI herb/spice category. This is a conservative analogous estimate and should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high potassium (4466 mg/100g) with moderate sodium (211 mg/100g); at culinary doses of 1–2g, sodium contribution is negligible (<5 mg) and potassium contribution is modest but positive.
hypertension.guidance: Dried coriander leaf is suitable for individuals with hypertension when used in typical culinary amounts (1–5g per serving); its exceptional potassium-to-sodium ratio supports DASH dietary principles, and the negligible per-serving sodium load poses no clinically meaningful concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (10.4g/100g), low available glycemic load at culinary doses, and evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties via alpha-glucosidase enzyme inhibition.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried coriander leaf is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; used in culinary quantities of 1–5g, the glycemic contribution is negligible, and phytochemical constituents including flavonoids and terpenoids have demonstrated hypoglycaemic activity in peer-reviewed studies, supporting its use as a functional flavouring herb.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.46g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and polyphenol-rich antioxidant profile supporting endothelial and lipid health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried coriander leaf is cardioprotective at culinary doses; its near-zero saturated fat and cholesterol content, combined with high potassium and antioxidant polyphenols, make it a heart-healthy seasoning consistent with AHA dietary guidelines for cardiovascular disease prevention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Polyphenol-rich anti-inflammatory herb; negligible saturated fat and absent trans fat qualify it as an anti-inflammatory spice.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried coriander leaf is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its polyphenolic and terpenoid content confers anti-inflammatory properties, and patients on anticoagulant therapy should maintain consistent rather than variable herb intake to avoid pharmacokinetic interactions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried coriander leaf (Coriandrum sativum) consists of dehydrated green coriander leaves processed into small flakes or crumbles. The drying process concentrates nutrients dramatically, yielding an exceptionally high potassium content of 4466 mg per 100g, calcium at 1246 mg per 100g, and iron at 42.46 mg per 100g — values far exceeding most common vegetables on a per-weight basis. The herb provides 279 kcal per 100g with 21.93g protein, 4.78g fat, and 52.1g carbohydrate, though these figures are nutritionally relevant only in the context of the very small culinary serving sizes of 1–5g typically used. The flavour profile is characteristically citrusy, slightly floral, and aromatic, derived from linalool and other volatile terpenes. Texture is dry and crisp. Dried coriander leaf is used globally as a garnish and flavouring in South Asian curries, Middle Eastern stews, Mexican salsas, Southeast Asian soups, and Mediterranean dishes. It is distinct from coriander seed, which has a different flavour and nutrient profile. Clinically, dried coriander leaf is low-GI, anti-inflammatory, and has demonstrated hypoglycaemic properties in peer-reviewed research. It is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. The herb is also known as dried cilantro leaf in North American culinary contexts and as dried dhania in South Asian contexts.

What is the difference between dried coriander leaf and coriander seed?
Is dried cilantro the same as dried coriander leaf?
Is dried coriander leaf good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 02013), an approved primary source with an exact match for dried coriander (cilantro) leaves. Input HPB values are concordant with USDA data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category framework, clearly documented as such.
notes: trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — dried herb with no industrial processing). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived food). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived food with no animal-derived components). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — dried plant leaf with negligible UV-synthesised vitamin D). The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input record has been removed as it is not applicable to a dried herb leaf. Saturated fat (0.46g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.03g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.95g/100g) values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and are consistent with the total fat of 4.78g/100g. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for dried coriander leaf was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches; SNOMED CT code 412061001 is an approximate match at plant substance level, with 'closest_parent' match type. Local-language names in local_names are set to null as this is a globally distributed generic herb ingredient, not a cuisine-specific dish requiring mandatory multilingual naming under G6 rules.
